verdicts.title: Broken File Extension Check in is_allowed_image_extension() Allows Any File Type
verdicts.attack_vector: The is_allowed_image_extension() function at Manager.php line 2113 has a variable name collision bug. Line 2114 assigns $extension = pathinfo($filename, PATHINFO_EXTENSION), but line 2119's foreach loop 'foreach ($allowed_extensions as $extension)' overwrites $extension with each allowed extension value. After the loop, $extension holds the last element of the original allowed list (e.g., 'webp'), which always passes the in_array() check on line 2124. The function therefore ALWAYS returns true regardless of the actual file extension. Verified with PHP runtime test: evil.php, evil.exe, and all other extensions pass the check. This completely disables file type validation for ZIP extraction (lines 196-201) and any other code path using this function.
verdicts.evidence: PHP runtime test confirmed: foreach ($allowed_extensions as $extension) overwrites $extension from pathinfo(). After loop, $extension='webp' regardless of input. in_array('webp', $allowed_extensions) always returns true. Files with .php, .exe, .html extensions all pass the check.
verdicts.remediation: Rename the foreach loop variable from $extension to $ext (or similar) to avoid overwriting the actual file extension: foreach ($allowed_extensions as $ext) { $allowed_extensions[] = $ext . '_backup'; }

verdicts.title: ZIP Slip Path Traversal in ZipArchive Extraction
verdicts.attack_vector: Manager.php lines 196-201 extract ZIP entries using ZipArchive::extractTo() without checking for path traversal sequences (../) in filenames. The only guard is is_allowed_image_extension() which is completely broken (see NGG-001). A crafted ZIP containing an entry like '../../wp-content/plugins/evil.php' would extract the file to arbitrary directories. Same issue exists in the PclZip fallback path at lines 209-219. Combined with NGG-001 (any file extension passes), an authenticated user with upload permissions can write any file type to any writable directory, achieving Remote Code Execution by placing a PHP webshell in a web-accessible directory.
verdicts.evidence: Line 197: $filename = $zipObj->getNameIndex($i); only checked by broken is_allowed_image_extension(). Line 201: $zipObj->extractTo($dest_path, [$zipObj->getNameIndex($i)]); no basename() or realpath() validation. Same pattern in PclZip path at line 213-216.
verdicts.remediation: Apply basename() to ZIP entry filenames before extraction, or validate that the resolved path is within $dest_path using realpath() after extraction. Fix is_allowed_image_extension() first (NGG-001).

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated Cookie Disclosure via cookie_dump AJAX Action
verdicts.attack_vector: The cookie_dump_action() method in A_NextGen_AddGallery_Ajax (package.module.nextgen_addgallery_page.php lines 100-108) returns all server-side $_COOKIE values as JSON without any authentication or nonce check. Accessible via GET/POST to /?photocrati_ajax=1&action=cookie_dump. Verified with curl: request with cookies returns {"success":1,"cookies":{"test_cookie":"secret_value"}}. While an attacker's own request only reflects their own cookies, this endpoint can be exploited in combination with an XSS vulnerability on the same origin to exfiltrate authenticated users' session cookies via fetch(). No CORS headers are set, limiting cross-origin exploitation.
verdicts.evidence: curl -s -b 'test_cookie=secret_value' 'http://localhost:8880/?photocrati_ajax=1&action=cookie_dump' returns {"success":1,"cookies":{"test_cookie":"secret_value"}}. No authentication, no nonce, no capability check.
verdicts.remediation: Add authentication and nonce verification to cookie_dump_action(), or remove the endpoint entirely as it appears to be a debugging leftover.

verdicts.title: SQL Injection via esc_sql() in Tag-Based Gallery Queries
verdicts.attack_vector: DisplayedGallery.php lines 1022-1037 construct a SQL IN() clause using esc_sql() with manual string concatenation instead of $wpdb->prepare() placeholders. Tag names from the 'tag_ids' shortcode attribute are escaped with esc_sql() and interpolated into the query string. The $wpdb->prepare() call on line 1045 only binds the taxonomy parameter, not the tag values. Under multibyte character sets (e.g., GBK, Big5), esc_sql() (which wraps mysqli_real_escape_string()) can be bypassed through multibyte character injection. An Author+ user who can create posts with shortcodes controls the tag names. The SQL executes on every unauthenticated front-end page view that renders the shortcode.
verdicts.auth_required: author (to create shortcode), exploitable on unauthenticated front-end
verdicts.evidence: Line 1023: $container = esc_sql(str_replace('%', '%%', $container)); Line 1024: $container_ids[] = "'{$container}'"; Line 1037: $query .= " AND ({$wpdb->terms}.slug IN ({$container_ids}) ..."; Line 1045: $query = $wpdb->prepare($query, 'ngg_tag'); -- only binds one %s placeholder, not the tag values.
verdicts.remediation: Replace the esc_sql() + string concatenation pattern with $wpdb->prepare() using individual %s placeholders for each tag value in the IN() clause.

verdicts.title: Client-Controlled MIME Type Determines ZIP Processing Path
verdicts.attack_vector: Manager.php is_zip() at lines 2191-2229 determines whether an uploaded file is a ZIP archive by reading $_FILES['file']['type'], which is the client-supplied Content-Type header. No magic bytes or file content inspection is performed. An attacker can force any upload to be processed through the ZIP extraction path by setting Content-Type to 'application/zip', or conversely bypass ZIP processing by using a different MIME type. Combined with NGG-001 (broken extension check) and NGG-002 (ZIP slip), this completes the attack chain: upload a crafted ZIP with Content-Type: application/zip containing a PHP webshell with path traversal.
verdicts.evidence: Line 2208: $type = $file_info['type']; -- directly from $_FILES, attacker-controlled. Lines 2212-2219: MIME type string matching determines if ZIP extraction occurs. No finfo_open() or magic byte check.
verdicts.remediation: Use finfo_open(FILEINFO_MIME_TYPE) or wp_check_filetype() with actual file content to determine file type instead of trusting the client-supplied Content-Type header.

verdicts.title: Custom AJAX Dispatcher Without Global Authentication Gate
verdicts.attack_vector: The custom AJAX system (M_Ajax::serve_ajax_request + C_Ajax_Controller::index_action) dispatches to action methods based on user-supplied input without any global authentication check. Each individual action method is responsible for calling validate_ajax_request(). This design pattern is error-prone: any new action method that forgets to check auth (like cookie_dump_action in NGG-003) is automatically accessible unauthenticated. The C_Ajax_Controller also uses dynamic method dispatch ($this->call_method("{$action}_action")) based on user input, which could expose methods from any mixin class registered as an adapter.
verdicts.evidence: Line 42-46: $action = $this->param('action'); $method = "{$action}_action"; if ($this->has_method($method)) { $retval = $this->call_method($method); } -- No auth check before dispatch. Each action must self-validate.
verdicts.remediation: Add a global authentication check (at minimum is_user_logged_in()) at the dispatcher level in index_action() before calling any action method. Individual action methods should still verify specific capabilities.

verdicts.title: Unsafe unserialize() on Database-Stored Data with Bypass Potential
verdicts.attack_vector: Serializable::unserialize() at src/Util/Serializable.php line 54 calls PHP's native unserialize() with allowed_classes => false on data starting with 'a:'. The check_for_serialized_objects() regex on line 76 checks for O:/C: patterns, but uses the original $value while the 'a:' check uses the stripcslashes'd $retval. The allowed_classes => false parameter prevents object instantiation, which is the primary defense. However, the function operates on database-stored data (image metadata, gallery settings) that could be poisoned if an attacker gains SQL write access. The defense-in-depth is adequate for the current threat model, but the use of native unserialize() is still a code smell.
verdicts.auth_required: admin (requires ability to modify database content)
verdicts.evidence: Line 54: $retval = @\unserialize($value, ['allowed_classes' => false]); Called from 14+ files across datamapper, image, album, and settings code. Data source is always database-stored values, not direct user input.
verdicts.remediation: Consider migrating all serialized data to JSON format. The 'allowed_classes => false' parameter provides adequate protection against POP chains, but json_decode is inherently safer.

verdicts.title: Plugin Activation/Deactivation REST Endpoints Without Plugin Allowlist
verdicts.attack_vector: PluginManagementREST.php provides REST endpoints at imagely/v1/plugins/activate and imagely/v1/plugins/deactivate that accept any plugin basename. While properly gated by current_user_can('activate_plugins'), there is no allowlist restricting which plugins can be managed. An administrator (or a compromised admin session) could use these endpoints to activate or deactivate security plugins (e.g., Wordfence, Sucuri) to weaken site defenses before further exploitation.
verdicts.evidence: Routes accept arbitrary plugin basename without allowlist. Permission check is current_user_can('activate_plugins') only.
verdicts.remediation: Add an allowlist restricting which plugins can be activated/deactivated through this endpoint to only Imagely-affiliated plugins.

verdicts.title: Settings REST Endpoint Accepts Arbitrary Keys Without Allowlist
verdicts.attack_vector: SettingsREST.php update_settings() at the PUT imagely/v1/settings endpoint accepts an arbitrary JSON object and stores all keys without an allowlist. An admin user could inject unexpected settings keys that influence plugin behavior in unintended ways. HTML values pass through wp_kses_post() which may permit certain JavaScript vectors in older WordPress versions.
verdicts.evidence: Accepts arbitrary JSON keys from request body and stores them as plugin settings without validation against an expected keys list.
verdicts.remediation: Implement a settings key allowlist to reject unknown setting names.

verdicts.title: XSS via REQUEST_URI in Admin Page Template - Properly Sanitized (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: The taint flow identifies $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] echoed in admin templates. However, the output is properly sanitized: nextgen_admin_page.php line 45 uses Router::esc_url(sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash($request_uri))), and display_settings_form.php line 4 uses esc_url(sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash($_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']))). Both esc_url() and sanitize_text_field() together provide adequate XSS protection for URL context output.
verdicts.evidence: echo \Imagely\NGG\Util\Router::esc_url(sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash($request_uri))); -- double sanitized with sanitize_text_field + esc_url.

verdicts.title: XSS via absint() Echo in Templates (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: Multiple taint flows flag echo absint($value) as XSS. absint() returns an absolute integer value which by definition cannot contain HTML/JS characters. This is a safe sanitization for numeric output.
verdicts.evidence: echo absint($gallery->counter); echo absint($notifications_count); -- absint() guarantees integer output, no XSS possible.

verdicts.title: RCE Taint Flows via array_map('intval') Sinks (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: The automated taint analysis flagged array_map('intval', ...) as an RCE sink because escapeshellarg() was not applied. However, array_map('intval') is a data sanitization step that casts all values to integers, not a command execution function. The resulting integer arrays are used in SQL queries with proper prepare() statements. No shell command execution occurs anywhere in these flows.
verdicts.evidence: $attachment_ids = array_map('intval', $attachment_ids); $int_ids = array_filter(array_map('intval', (array)$image_ids)); -- intval casting is sanitization, not a dangerous sink.

verdicts.title: RCE Taint Flows via preg_replace and String Operations (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: Multiple taint flows flag preg_replace(), usort(), array_map('trim'), array_map('sanitize_title'), and other string manipulation functions as RCE sinks because 'escapeshellarg() not used'. These are string processing functions, not shell command execution. preg_replace with /e modifier was removed in PHP 7.0 and none of these patterns use the /e flag. The automated tool appears to have a high false positive rate for this pattern, flagging any function that processes user input as an RCE sink.
verdicts.evidence: preg_replace('/^/?wp-content#/', '', $retval); preg_replace('/^(ngg|ngg_images) /i', '', $shortcode, 1); usort($notifications, function($a,$b){...}); -- none of these are shell execution functions.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via Content-Type Header (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: The taint flows flag header('Content-Type: ...') calls as open redirect vectors. Content-Type headers do not cause browser redirects. The values come from internal _content_type property or are hardcoded strings ('text/plain'). get_option('blog_charset') returns a WP database setting that is not directly user-controllable via normal request flow.
verdicts.evidence: header('Content-Type: ' . $this->object->_content_type . '; charset=' . get_option('blog_charset'), true); -- Content-Type headers cannot redirect.

verdicts.title: SSRF via Onboarding Wizard Email Subscription (False Positive)
verdicts.attack_vector: The taint flow flags wp_safe_remote_post() as SSRF because user input (email) flows into the request body. However: (1) the email is sanitized with sanitize_email(), (2) it flows into the POST body, not the URL, (3) the URL is hardcoded to the Imagely/Drip API endpoint, and (4) wp_safe_remote_post() already blocks private IP ranges. The user-controlled data cannot influence the target URL.
verdicts.evidence: $email = sanitize_email($onboarding_data['_email_address']); ... $response = wp_safe_remote_post($url, $args); -- $url is hardcoded, $email goes into POST body only.

verdicts.title: Variable Injection via parse_str in RoutingApp (Low Risk)
verdicts.attack_vector: RoutingApp.php line 594 uses parse_str($parsed, $params) on the query string from REQUEST_URI. The second argument catches the parsed variables into $params array (not global scope), limiting the impact. However, the parsed parameters could potentially override expected routing parameters if not properly validated downstream. The REQUEST_URI is sanitized through Router::sanitize_request_uri_for_routing() which strips control characters and HTML tags.
verdicts.evidence: $parsed = wp_parse_url(Router::sanitize_request_uri_for_routing($_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']), PHP_URL_QUERY); parse_str($parsed, $params); -- parse_str into $params array, not global scope.
verdicts.remediation: Validate expected parameter names after parse_str or use explicit parameter extraction instead.

summary.most_critical_finding: NGG-001 + NGG-002: Broken file extension validation (is_allowed_image_extension always returns true due to variable name collision) combined with ZIP slip path traversal enables authenticated RCE. An Author-level user can upload a crafted ZIP file containing a PHP webshell with directory traversal paths, achieving arbitrary file write and code execution.
summary.exec_system_assessment: The initial report of 4 files with exec/system was misleading. No shell_exec(), exec(), system(), or passthru() calls exist in the PHP code. The matches were: (1) WP_Filesystem() calls (WordPress filesystem API, not shell execution), (2) JavaScript RegExp.exec() in admin templates, (3) Router::passthru() which is a routing method, not PHP's passthru() shell function.
summary.unserialize_assessment: The 11 files with unserialize all route through Serializable::unserialize() which uses allowed_classes => false and has a regex check for serialized objects. The defense is adequate. Data comes from database, not direct user input.
summary.ajax_architecture_concern: The plugin uses a custom AJAX dispatcher (photocrati_ajax) that dispatches to action methods without a global auth gate. Individual methods must self-validate. The cookie_dump_action demonstrates the risk of this pattern -- it was shipped without any auth check.
